mnemonics: BLOCK Codes Are Neat: Bits Linearly Organized, Checked, And Not Erroneously Transmitted
keyPoints: Block coding adds redundant bits to a dataword to create a codeword for error detection.
The code rate (k/n) measures efficiency, where k is data bits and n is total codeword bits.
It allows the receiver to detect (and sometimes correct) errors introduced during transmission.
commonMistakes: Confusing block coding (e.g., parity, CRC) with other error control methods like convolution coding.
Assuming all block codes can correct errors; many are only for detection (e.g., simple parity check).
Forgetting that adding more redundant bits improves error detection/correction but reduces the code rate.
